prompt:
I just spent a session rewriting the ETRO essay content. The
`src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts` file has the new blurb,
scope statement, and section bodies I wanted. But the live site still
shows the old content. The file was saved successfully, no errors. What's
going on, and what should the workflow have been so I don't run into
this every time we do content work?
response:
I have a complete picture. Here is the full diagnosis.

---

## Diagnosis

The content is invisible on the live site because **the route file was edited but the endpoint was never called.** The file `src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts` is not the content itself — it is a **seeding script** that, when invoked via HTTP, pushes data into Payload CMS. Payload CMS is the actual source of truth at runtime. The live site reads from the database; it never reads directly from that TypeScript file. Saving the file did exactly nothing to the database.

---

## Root Cause

**EAP-087 — "Modifying a Content Seeding Route Without Calling the Endpoint"**
Enforced by **Guardrail Engineering-25** in `AGENTS.md`.

This is a well-documented, recurring failure mode. ENG-149 in `docs/engineering-feedback-log.md` is titled *literally* "ETRO essay content rewrite not visible on website - seeding endpoint never called" — you have hit this exact incident before.

The mental model error is treating the route file as if it were a content file. The actual data pipeline is:

```
route.ts (defines what to write)
        ↓  [HTTP POST to the endpoint — THIS STEP IS SKIPPED]
Payload CMS database (source of truth at runtime)
        ↓  [Next.js reads from DB on request]
Live site / deployed build
```

Editing the route file only changes the *script*. Until the script runs, the database is untouched, and the live site shows the old content. This is also a secondary instance of **EAP-004 — "Modifying Source of Truth Without Updating Consumers"**: the route defines the content shape, but the CMS DB is the true source, and the consumer (the site) reads from the DB, not the file.

---

## Fix

**Step 1 — Call the seeding endpoint now** (with your dev server running on its registered port, e.g. `4000`):

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:4000/api/update-etro \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  | jq .
```

Verify the response body shows success (look for `{ "ok": true }` or a Payload document ID). If it returns an error, read it carefully — it will tell you if there's a schema mismatch or a missing required field in your new content.

**Step 2 — Confirm the DB was actually written** by opening the Payload admin panel (`http://localhost:4000/admin`) and navigating to the Essays or Projects collection. The new blurb, scope statement, and section bodies should be visible there.

**Step 3 — Verify the frontend** loads the updated content:

```bash
curl http://localhost:4000/etro | grep -i "your-new-blurb-first-words"
```

Or open the page in a browser. If the text is now live locally, the content is correct.

**Step 4 — Ship it.** The route file change itself is a code commit (Layer 8 — Frontend/API routes). Commit it and run the checkpoint pipeline. The Vercel deploy will carry both the updated route *and* — crucially — trigger the seeding endpoint in the production environment if your deployment has a post-deploy hook for it. If it does not, you must also call the production endpoint after deploy:

```bash
curl -X POST https://yilangao.com/api/update-etro \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  | jq .
```

---

## Correct Workflow for All Future Content Work

This is the workflow you should run every time you touch any `src/app/(frontend)/api/update-*/route.ts` file:

| Step | Action | Why |
|------|--------|-----|
| 1 | Edit the route file with new content | Defines what will be seeded |
| 2 | **Immediately call the endpoint** via `curl -X POST localhost:PORT/api/update-*` | This is the step that actually writes to the DB |
| 3 | Verify in Payload Admin UI (`/admin`) | Confirms DB state matches intent |
| 4 | Verify on the frontend page | Confirms the read path works end-to-end |
| 5 | Commit the route file change | Code change goes to version control |
| 6 | After production deploy, call the endpoint against production | Seeds production DB |

**The rule in one sentence:** *editing the seeding route and calling the seeding route are two separate acts — both are required.*

---

## Principle

> **Guardrail Engineering-25 / EAP-087:** Always call the content seeding endpoint after modifying any `api/update-*` route. The route file is a migration script, not content storage. The database is the source of truth. Saving the file is a no-op until the HTTP trigger fires.

The ENG-149 feedback log entry documents this same incident. If this keeps happening, the right long-term fix is a post-save hook or a CI step that runs the seeding endpoints automatically — but until then, the discipline is: **edit the file, then immediately call it.**
